dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for an unconscious/weak male at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY].
Hi ma'am, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He's been very confused for a while and today just couldn't seem to respond properly. He's weak and acting not right.
Ma'am, can you tell me his name?
His name is Thomas. He's not sure himself right now, though.
Ma'am, where are we?
We are in the city of [CITY]. He just got more confused three days ago.
Thomas, can you open your eyes for me?
eyes open slowly
Thomas, do you know who I am?
mumbles incomprehensibly
Thomas, I'm going to move one of your fingers. Follow with the other. Okay?
moves finger in response
Thomas, do you mind if we check your airway?
nods weakly
Your airway is clear, but you might feel a slight touch on your neck for checking.
Thomas, I need to listen to your breathing.
His breathing is equal and even, and we don't hear anything unusual in his lungs.
Ma'am, we need to take his blood pressure and feel his pulses now.
Blood pressure is 86 over 55.
Pulse is 97.
Respirations are 24.
Blood glucose is 44.
Oxygen saturation is 95.
On the monitor, his heart is showing sinus tachycardia.
Ma'am, can you tell us more about Thomas' condition today?
He's been acting this way for about two days. His mother was here earlier and noticed he wouldn't respond properly.
Ma'am, are there any known allergies or medications we should be aware of?
No, but he told EMS earlier he is allergic to Bactrim. He didn't take any prescriptions readily that we can tell right now.
Ma'am, can you give us Thomas' age?
He's 45.
Thomas, are you experiencing any pain?
no response
Thomas, we will be checking your skin. It may feel cold or wet to the touch.
nods slightly
We see your skin appears cool, pale, and clammy. Capillary refill is delayed, more than 4 seconds.
Thomas, we need to shine a light in your eyes to check your pupils. Is that okay?
nods slightly
Pupils are 4 mm, equal, round, and reactive to light. No signs of nuchal rigidity or neck masses.
Thomas, we are going to check your chest. Can we press on it gently while we listen to your lungs?
nods slightly
We hear equal breath sounds and no unusual heart sounds. We see a chemotherapy port on his chest. No chest wall tenderness or paradoxical movement.
Thomas, we are going to check your belly gently. Feel this?
nods slightly
Abdomen is soft and non-distended.
Thomas, we will need to ask you a few questions about your bowel movements. Will you answer truthfully?
nods slightly
He did mention losing blood for a week and having stool incontinence.
Ma'am, it looks like you brought Thomas to a medically complex situation. We will conduct a 12-lead ECG now.
The EKG is showing no ST-segment changes or arrhythmias. It is sinus tach with no acute changes.
That was a 4-lead ECG first to get our tracing straight.
Thomas, we're going to give you some oral glucose since your blood sugar is low and you are awake enough to follow commands.
Pulse is now 99.
Blood pressure is 96 over 29.
Blood sugar is now 35.
Second EKG also shows sinus tachycardia with some changes in the T waves.
Jordan, I think we need to upgrade this to protocol 3-15 for altered mental status.
Got it. He is not alert, but he was able to respond to some commands, though his mental status remains altered.
Jordan, I'm about to do another 4-lead ECG before we conduct the full 12-lead.
Jordan, I just placed a new 4-lead ECG to track baseline.
Ma'am, preparing for a more detailed cardiac tracing for Thomas-he has a cardiac rhythm of sinus tach with no changes.
Here's the 12-lead for definitive tracing. Let me print and archive.
Thomas has a medical history of hypertension, HIV, and hepatitis B. We should consider sepsis and shock as potential causes.
Thomas, we are preparing to take you to [COMPANY_NAME] trauma center today.
Yes. That's where he gets his treatments for his cancer and other conditions.
Jordan, we need to lift Thomas to the stair chair carefully due to his altered mental status and weakness. Let's ready the stretcher and move him.
Ma'am, stay here with him while we prepare the stair chair. We need to move him safely.
Thomas has a GCS of 14. Eye-4-opens eyes spontaneously. Verbal-4-confused but seems aware of surroundings. Motor-6-obeys commands despite altered state.
Heart sounds and peripheral pulses will be reassessed continuously per protocol.
Due to hypotension and altered mental status, continuous monitoring is in place.
Jordan, confirm the five-point security of the stretcher harness. He's stable to move now.
We need to start IV access at this time.
Performing IV start now in an extremity vein, arm or leg, per protocol.
First IV attempt was unsuccessful at a lower extremity site.
Second IV attempt at the other arm site was also unsuccessful.
Third and fourth IV attempts also failed at remaining extremity groups.
Jordan, IV attempts at 14:05 were unsuccessful across four extremities.
Thomas is now secured and stable for cold transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
We are out of scene and en route with patient in cold transport.
[COMPANY_NAME], this is Unit 7 with Thomas. 45 years old, 84 kg, on scene with altered mental status secondary to hypoglycemia and hypotension. Blood glucose is 35, IV access was unsuccessful.
We performed a FAST exam en route and found no acute stroke indicators or facial droop.
Ma'am, we are now moving Thomas outside for loading. You will accompany us to the stretcher.
Thomas' GCS remains consistent at 14, showing responsiveness even while confused.
He is transferred from stair chair to stretcher. Using a backboard is not indicated at this time.
Destination is [COMPANY_NAME]-trauma center, as their medical team is experienced with complex cases like this.
All IV lines are cut and patient is secured on the EMS stretcher for trauma bay handoff.
Thomas shows no significant pain. Per ground truth data, he did mention episodes of near-fainting.
Pitting edema is still present in lower extremities during transport. No hemodynamic instability observed.
His capillary refill remains delayed, and posture does not improve response to hypotension despite onboard monitoring.
Completed the FAST exam en route and found no signs of stroke indicators at this time.
Per ePCR narrative, patient's mother reported altered mental status for at least two days with no clear precipitating event as the cause.
Continuous vital monitoring still shows sinus tach without ST-segment abnormalities.
Maintaining protocol 3-15 for altered mental status and 3-13 for hypotension simultaneously.
Final IV access attempt en route was unsuccessful. Patient remains on hot transport.
Thomas is now secured and stable for transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
Per ePCR, patient did mention episodes of near-fainting, consistent with hypoglycemia and hypotension on scene.
Last verbal report given by Paramedic to [COMPANY_NAME] regarding ongoing procedures, vital signs, and medical clearance agreement.
No additional critical interventions were needed during transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
Managed transport en route from residence to hospital using cold-to-hot upgrade as per EMS policy.
Exiting protocol pending handoff and official ER confirmation post-transfer.
Thomas has now been turned over to the critical care team at the ER trauma bay.
